title: Bleeding
summary: Severe bleeding can lead to shock and death within minutes. Immediate action to control blood flow is critical.
steps: Protect yourself — wear gloves if available to avoid infection.
Apply firm, direct pressure on the wound with a clean cloth or bandage.
Maintain continuous pressure for at least 10 minutes without peeking.
If blood soaks through, add more cloth on top — do NOT remove the first layer.
Elevate the injured area above the level of the heart if possible.
If bleeding is from a limb and uncontrollable, consider applying a tourniquet above the wound.
Keep the person still, warm, and calm. Monitor for shock.
what_to_do: Apply and maintain firm direct pressure
Keep the person calm and lying down
Use clean cloth or bandage to cover wound
Watch for signs of shock (pale, cold, rapid breathing)
what_not_to_do: Do NOT remove the cloth if soaked — add more on top
Do NOT probe or explore the wound
Do NOT apply a tourniquet unless absolutely necessary
Do NOT give food or water to a severely bleeding person
Do NOT leave the person alone
when_to_call_ambulance: Bleeding does not stop after 10–15 minutes of pressure
Deep wound or wound that exposes bone or muscle
Wound is from a large or sharp object
Signs of shock: pale, cold, faint, rapid weak pulse
